title: What an American thinks about Denmark - August 2010 One week

transcript.text: Hello, this video is what an American thinks about Denmark.
I was in Denmark maybe like a week.
I rode my bike from Sweden, from Stockholm to Copenhagen,
So I went across the northern tip of that island
And then rode down to Copenhagen.
And I stayed in Copenhagen like three nights.
One night in a hostel and two nights camping.
And then I rode away.
One day to the edge and to the southern tip of the next island
And then I took the boat to Germany.
So never went to the mainland of Denmark, but Denmark, very expensive.
I think it's more expensive than Sweden.
Maybe a little bit, but Sweden's expensive too.
The McDonald's have free internet, which is cool.
All over Europe except for Germany and like Switzerland
And, you know, don't have it free internet there.
But bike paths everywhere.
More so than Sweden, Norway, and Finland.
Denmark is like the Netherlands.
I rode bike paths, well, not as much as the Netherlands, but close.
Because I did ride on the roads, but I did do a lot of riding on bike paths
When I was in Denmark.
A lot of biking, like a big biking city.
Copenhagen is probably the most biking-packed city I've ever seen,
Even more than Amsterdam.
I mean, bikes everywhere.
Bike paths just jacked, jacked stock full of bikers.
Bikers stop at a red light and you see like 30 bikers there.
And then it goes and it's a whole pack.
Yeah, more than Amsterdam.
I mean, people biking like crazy out there.
Just around the town bikes, you know.
But they go fast too, faster than the Dutch.
I think the Dutch are stoned.
And even though somebody says that, you know, only 20% of Dutch have smoked weed in their life
And 40% of Americans have.
I don't know if that's true.
A Dutch person said that on a YouTube video.
But yeah, Swedes were going pretty fast.
But Copenhagen is really pretty.
It's right on the sea.
They have this really beautiful church with this like spiral staircase that goes around the edge of it.
And it's like you can infertile as you go on the top, which I thought was really cool.
A pain of gold.
And then they have this like open green space with paths going through it that's right next to the downtown area of Copenhagen
That I could camp in because the grass was five feet high.
And they look like the Swedes and the Norwegians.
They're not as fat as the Swedes.
Swedes were fat, generally, compared to, well, comparatively.
And, but I think the Danes were taller.
It seemed like when I got to Denmark, there was like tall people around.
Not as tall as the Dutch, though.
The Dutch are giants.
And let's see.
Yeah, and then I rode down a lot of bike paths.
And yeah, friendly, outgoing people, too.
Weed's illegal there, pretty much.
Well, it's illegal, but you can buy weed like right on the street.
You see weed everywhere when you go to this little suburb called Christiana.
And it's funny because when you get the map of the city, you know, it's like here's the city and here's the, you know, with all the names of the streets.
You look at Christiana, which I heard is the second most visited place in all of Copenhagen after, I don't, I can't remember.
After like, oh, after like their amusement park that they have right there in the middle.
But it's called Pusher Street, you know, Pusher, like a drug dealer.
That's what it's officially called on the map and on the road.
And you go in and there's like marijuana and hash all over the place on tables.
But it's illegal.
Like the cops would come every once in a while and like try and bust them.
But they'd pick up it in the little, pick it up in the little, you know, table closet they're on and throw it on the roof and like hide it.

And like, you know, till the cops go away, you know, but, um, but, uh, yeah, it was the only place other than Amsterdam or unlike country other than the Netherlands and Copenhagen where you can actually buy stuff on the street.
It's really cool.
And, um, yeah, um, Copenhagen is very beautiful.
It's right on the edge of the city, uh, the, the sea.
They have, um, like this, like standing river, like our slow moving big river going right through the middle of it.
And then next to it, they have this other, pretty much look like a river, these huge, like lakes, perfectly like rectangle, but one after the other.
So it's kind of like a city with two rivers going through it.
Instead of just one, you have two.
I think it's the only city where I've seen lists like that.
So that's really cool.
I guess in that respect, similar to Amsterdam, although, you know, Amsterdam's got canals freaking everywhere.
It's insane.
Like every street's like a canal and I'm loving Amsterdam, but Denmark, uh, was really cool.
I liked Denmark really friendly to this one guy, you know, at a rainbow gathering, Finland,
It was a Dane invited me over to hang out with him.
Um, you know, eat mushrooms and stuff.
And, and, uh, so yeah, Denmark is cool, but that's about all I have to say about that.
